root_cause: Health checks depend on an unavailable upstream dependency, causing probe failures and restart pressure
evidence: Timeline shows dependency failure before readiness probe failures and restart pressure for container 'api'
Observed 2 dependency -> probe failure -> restart chain(s) within the recent incident window
Container 'api' now has restartCount=5, ready=False, state=waiting
Dependency service 'postgres' has no ready endpoints, reinforcing the upstream outage signal

causes.message: Recent failures show dependency 'postgres.default.svc.cluster.local:5432' is unavailable before probe failures begin
Application health checks depend on external dependency availability instead of local process health
Readiness probes fail as the dependency outage propagates through the health endpoint
The pod becomes unstable or restarts as dependency-driven health failures accumulate

pod:orders-api-6b5d7d8c96-jq5kh: Readiness health checks are failing downstream of dependency unavailability
container:api: Error: failed to connect to postgres.default.svc.cluster.local:5432: dial tcp 10.96.42.17:5432: connect: connection refused
Readiness probe failed: GET /readyz returned 503 because dependency postgres.default.svc.cluster.local:5432 is unavailable
Back-off restarting failed container api in pod orders-api-6b5d7d8c96-jq5kh_default
service:postgres: Dependency service has no ready endpoints during the probe failure chain

likely_causes: The readiness or liveness endpoint is coupled to database or cache availability instead of local container health
An upstream dependency outage is propagating into probe failures because the health check is too deep
The application exits or degrades when the dependency is unavailable, turning an upstream outage into restart churn
suggested_checks: Inspect the health endpoint implementation and remove hard dependency checks from liveness where possible
Verify dependency availability and service endpoints for the upstream database or backing service
